front: Building: Philadelphia house designed for the architect's mother Vanna; exposed chimney, wrap-around staircase, sloping roof, monumental split-gable facade; called 'the first Postmodern anything'
Building: Venturi lived in this house after marrying Denise Scott Brown; located near Louis Kahn's Esherick House
Book: 1972 architectural manifesto co-authored with Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour; celebrated Las Vegas roadside decor; introduced the distinction between 'ducks' and 'decorated sheds'
Book: full title is 'A Significance for A&P Parking Lots, or Learning from Las Vegas'; genesis came from a study of A&P parking lots
Book: Venturi led Yale students on helicopter tours of Las Vegas dubbed the 'Great Proletarian Cultural Locomotive'; described neon sign movement as 'quicker than mosaic glitter'
Book: its publication split architects into 'Greys' (followers) and 'Whites' (dissenters); the Whites included New York Five architects Richard Meier and Michael Graves
Book: contains a section titled 'Space as God'; uses La Tourette Monastery as an example
Book: 1966 text calling for an eclectic 'messy vitality' against Modernist purity; attacked Mies van der Rohe's minimalism
back: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (Robert Venturi)

front: Book: 'nonstraightforward' introduction presents a 'gentle manifesto'; cites Gödel's incompleteness theorem and T.S. Eliot's analysis of 'difficult' poetry as precedents
back: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (Robert Venturi)

front: Book: analyzes 'double-functioning' and 'both-and' elements of buildings; argues buildings should use 'poche' instead of transparency
back: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (Robert Venturi)

front: Book: praises Philip Johnson's Wiley House but bemoans the 'bland architecture' of his Glass House; praises Alvar Aalto for combining 'standardization with irrationality' in his Wolfsburg Cultural Center
back: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (Robert Venturi)

front: Book: Yale historian Vincent Scully wrote the introduction
back: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (Robert Venturi)
Fire Station No. 4, Columbus, Indiana (Robert Venturi)

front: Building: fire station with a giant gold '4' at the top; hose-drying tower in the middle makes the whole structure resemble an overturned number four
back: Fire Station No. 4, Columbus, Indiana (Robert Venturi)

front: Building: financed by J. Irwin Miller in Columbus, Indiana, nicknamed 'Athens on the Prairie'
back: Fire Station No. 4, Columbus, Indiana (Robert Venturi)

front: Building: addition to London's National Gallery co-designed with Denise Scott Brown; Scott Brown refused to attend the 1991 Pritzker Prize ceremony because Venturi received sole credit
Architect: 'Less is a bore' — quip playing on Mies van der Rohe's 'Less is more'; leading figure of Postmodern architecture
Architect: Louis Kahn was his teacher; both worked in Philadelphia
Building: Lieb House — semicircle 'sailboat' window, number nine beside the front door; had to be floated up the East River to escape New Jersey
Building: Franklin Court in Philadelphia — two steel-tube 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes
Architect: Denise Scott Brown's essay 'Room at the Top? Sexism and the Star System in Architecture' criticized the field for crediting him alone with coining the term 'duck building'
Architect: his favorite building in America was Sever Hall by H.H. Richardson at Harvard
Building: designed a bank in the Disney-sponsored planned town of Celebration, Florida, alongside a movie theatre by Cesar Pelli
